title: Dog Cherry Eye: Red Lump in Eye Corner Treatment & Triage
meta_desc: Find out what a cherry red lump in your dog's eye means. Learn about Cherry Eye symptoms, urgency levels, and why a photo is vital for vet triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: A red lump in your dog's eye is likely "Cherry Eye," a prolapsed tear gland that requires veterinary attention within 24-48 hours to prevent long-term complications like chronic dry eye.

What is the red lump appearing in my dog's eye?

A cherry-red lump appearing in the inner corner of your dog's eye is most likely a prolapsed gland of the third eyelid, commonly referred to as 'Cherry Eye.' Dogs have a third eyelid that houses a significant tear-producing gland. When the connective tissue holding this gland in place weakens or fails, the gland pops out, appearing as a fleshy, red, bulbous mass.

Is a red lump in my dog's eye a veterinary emergency?

  • Cherry Eye is a medium-urgency condition that will not resolve on its own.
  • The exposed gland can become dry, irritated, and prone to secondary infections.
  • If left untreated, it can lead to long-term issues like 'dry eye' (keratoconjunctivitis sicca).
  • You should contact your veterinarian to schedule an exam within 24 to 48 hours.

How does taking a photo of my dog's eye help with triage?

  • A clear, high-resolution photo allows the vet to see the exact state of the gland.
  • Veterinary teams can check for signs of discharge or corneal cloudiness.
  • Photos help monitor if the size of the lump changes over time.
  • This visual data helps the clinic determine how quickly your pet needs to be seen and helps them prepare diagnostic tools before you arrive.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In young dogs, especially breeds such as American Cocker Spaniels, Beagles, Lhasa Apsos, Pekingese, and English Bulldogs, hypertrophy, inflammation, and prolapse of the gland of the nictitating membrane (cherry eye) is common. Acutely, this presents as a red glandular mass protruding over the leading edge of the nictitans, often with mucopurulent discharge. Because the gland is a major tear producer, preservation is paramount; therefore, complete excision should be avoided as it predisposes the patient to keratoconjunctivitis sicca later in life. Treatment involves surgical replacement and anchoring of the gland with sutures to the orbital rim, periorbital fascia, or nictitans cartilage, or covering it with adjacent mucosa using envelope or pocket techniques. Following surgical or medical resolution, these dogs should be monitored for keratoconjunctivitis sicca for several years.

Chapter: Ophthalmology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 490)

faqs.question: Can I treat Cherry Eye at home with eye drops?
faqs.answer: While some drops may reduce inflammation, they will not fix the underlying structural issue. Professional veterinary treatment, often surgery, is usually required to reposition the gland.

faqs.question: Is Cherry Eye painful for my dog?
faqs.answer: Initially, it may not be painful, but it can be uncomfortable. If the dog rubs at the eye, they can cause painful corneal ulcers or infections.

faqs.question: Which dog breeds are most prone to this?
faqs.answer: Brachycephalic (flat-faced) breeds like Bulldogs, Pugs, and Frenchies, as well as Beagles and Cocker Spaniels, are genetically more susceptible to this condition.
